metadata.title:
SPOKANE INDIANS BELIEVE IN,


ssLET THE WOMEN DO THE WORK.

pages.raw_text:
stand the Indian language she was
excluded from the conversation.
However, the mystery was solved
in few days when the Indian girl
came back to the house with her
hair rolled over a rat, or something
which served the purpose and
coiled up. on top of her head.


Jewelry seems to be their hobby
and rarely If ever you will see a
squaw without earrings and they
delight in bedecking themselves
with all kinds of cheap rings and
bracelets.


One evening a party Of white
people strolled over to the camps.
The air was still and quiet and
the clouds of dusk were gathering.
AS we approached a tent We heard
strains of weird Indian music. We
listened a moment and then moved
toward the entrance and peered in.
Three Indians were stretched out
on blankets on the floor, evidently
taking life easy, while in one cor
ner on an old box, the only stick
of furniture in the tent, sat Bill,
an old Indian playing on fiddle.


He smiled at us through the
twilight and then began playing
Red Wing" Although he made
several mistakes, by which you
could tell he was playing by ear,
it seemed that Red Wing" was
never so realistic before and when
we applauded him he semed de-
lighted and he started to play Let
the Women Do the Work." When
asked where he learned the tune.
he said, "Oh, l caught it down on
the river"


With a "so-long, come again."
from the old Indian, we departed.
